1.synthesis: God's final answer to Moses's 'uncircumcised lips' objection is a commission that reframes the entire confrontation: 'See, I have made you as God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron shall be your prophet' (v.1). Moses will speak what God commands; Aaron will speak it to Pharaoh; Pharaoh will not listen; and God will multiply his signs so that 'the Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH' (v.5). The hardening of Pharaoh's heart — 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart' — is introduced here as divine sovereign action within a context that makes clear throughout the plague narrative that Pharaoh also hardened his own heart (v.3). Moses is 80 years old, Aaron 83, when they stand before Pharaoh (v.7). The commission frames the entire plague sequence: this is not diplomacy but divine self-revelation through judgment.
1.historical_context: In Egyptian theology, Pharaoh was the divine son of Ra, living Horus incarnate. Moses being set 'as God to Pharaoh' positions him as the representative of the superior divine power — YHWH above Pharaoh's divine status. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart engages one of the most discussed theological problems in the OT: the text presents both divine hardening (7:3) and Pharaoh's own hardening of his heart (8:15, 32; 9:34), with the divine hardening becoming more prominent in the later plagues. The purpose stated explicitly is revelatory: 'the Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH' (v.5) — the plagues are YHWH's self-introduction to Egypt. The ages of Moses and Aaron (80 and 83) signal the completion of the preparatory phase: the long years in Midian are now ended.
1.literary_structure: The unit serves as the theological introduction to the plague narrative (chs.7–12). It establishes the framework that every subsequent plague encounter will follow: divine command → Moses and Aaron address Pharaoh → Pharaoh refuses → plague → partial reprieve → Pharaoh re-hardens. The 'multiplying of signs' (v.3) anticipates the escalating structure: each plague greater than the last, each refusal deeper than the previous, until the tenth plague breaks what all others could not. The purpose-statement — 'that you may know that I am YHWH' — will recur as a refrain throughout the plague narrative.
1.theological_application: Gill on the hardening: 'the hardening of Pharaoh's heart was not the immediate work of God, but a permission of it, or a providential one; God, by his various providences, gave Pharaoh occasion to harden his heart, which he did of himself and choice, and God only permitted it.' Matthew Henry: 'God here threatens the hardening of Pharaoh's heart, to prepare Moses for the difficulties he would meet with. When God works he will have it known that it is his work, and not man's.' The plague narrative is the most extended demonstration of divine sovereignty over human resistance in the OT, culminating in the knowledge of God that comes not through willing obedience but through experienced judgment.
1.christology: Moses set 'as God to Pharaoh' anticipates the pattern in which God's representative speaks the divine word to resistant power. Christ before Pilate stands in this tradition — the true God facing the representative of worldly power, speaking divine truth to a man who asks 'what is truth?' (John 18:38). Paul's 'the god of this world has blinded the minds of unbelievers' (2 Corinthians 4:4) applies the hardening-theology to the gospel's resistance: as Pharaoh's hardening served YHWH's revelatory purpose, so the world's resistance to the gospel serves the same end — 'that the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' might be revealed (2 Corinthians 4:6).

8.synthesis: When Pharaoh demands a sign, Aaron throws down his staff and it becomes a serpent. Pharaoh's wise men and sorcerers do likewise with their staffs — 'but Aaron's staff swallowed up their staffs' (v.12). Pharaoh's heart is hardened, and he does not listen. The serpent-staff contest is the first public confrontation between YHWH's power and Egypt's ritual-magical tradition. The Egyptian magicians' ability to replicate the sign (seemingly) is the occasion for the decisive difference: YHWH's serpent devours theirs. The contest is not merely impressive — it establishes the hierarchy of power before any plague begins. Egypt's magic can imitate but not overcome what YHWH does.
8.historical_context: The uraeus cobra on Pharaoh's crown was the emblem of divine royal protection. The serpent (tannin, a large serpent or sea-monster) Aaron's staff becomes may be the royal cobra itself — YHWH's rod devouring Egypt's symbol. The Egyptian magicians (hartummim) are identified in 2 Timothy 3:8 as 'Jannes and Jambres,' names preserved in Jewish tradition. Their ability to replicate the sign has been attributed to natural means (the mongoose-and-cobra trick, known in ancient Egypt) or demonic power — the text does not adjudicate. What matters narratively is that their power is limited: Aaron's staff consumes theirs. The sequence establishes the pattern of the plague narrative: Egyptian magic can imitate but cannot ultimately match divine power.
8.literary_structure: The snake-staff contest (vv.8–13) functions as a preview sign before the plagues proper begin. It establishes the Egyptian magicians as the primary counter-agents whose ability to replicate some plagues (blood, frogs) but not others (gnats, boils) charts the escalating breakdown of Egypt's power. The swallowing of the Egyptian staffs is the first tangible demonstration that YHWH's power is not merely equal to Egypt's but superior to and devouring of it. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart (v.13) after this unambiguous sign shows that the issue is not evidential but volitional — no sign will soften a heart set against God.
8.theological_application: Gill on Aaron's staff swallowing the others: 'which showed that the rod of Moses and Aaron, and the power of God with them, was superior to all the power and craft of the magicians, and of Satan that assisted them; and may be an emblem of the Gospel swallowing up all false doctrine.' Matthew Henry: 'Though the magicians could harden Pharaoh's heart, they could not soften it; and though they seemed to vie with the miracles of Moses, they could not disannul them. God will at last have the better of all his enemies.' The swallowing of the other staffs is a enacted parable of divine power over competing claims to authority.
8.christology: The Egyptian magicians in 2 Timothy 3:8 are cited as the type of 'men who resist the truth' whose 'folly will be plain to all' — the parallel is to false teachers who oppose the gospel as Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses. The devouring of their staffs is the prefigure of Christ's ultimate triumph over all competing spiritual powers: 'having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The serpent-on-the-staff that Moses later lifts in the wilderness (Numbers 21) and Jesus applies to his own lifting-up (John 3:14) continues the serpent-staff symbolism begun here.

14.pericope_label: First Plague: Water Turned to Blood
14.synthesis: Pharaoh's heart is hardened; YHWH sends Moses to meet him in the morning at the Nile. The announcement is the Exodus's recurring formula: 'By this you shall know that I am YHWH' (v.17). Aaron strikes the Nile with the staff, and the water turns to blood: fish die, the river stinks, Egyptians cannot drink, blood is throughout Egypt (vv.20–21). The magicians replicate the sign (on presumably unaffected water still available), giving Pharaoh grounds for continued hardness. For seven days the Nile is blood (v.25). The first plague strikes Egypt's most fundamental resource — the Nile, source of all Egyptian agricultural life, object of religious veneration as the deity Hapi — and declares it undrinkable.
14.historical_context: The Nile was Egypt's lifeblood — not merely a water source but the deity responsible for the annual inundation that fertilized Egypt's fields. The god Hapi personified the Nile's flood; the god Khnum was said to control the Nile's source. The blood-plague directly attacks Egypt's primary deity and its most essential resource. The plague's timing — at the Nile, where Moses meets Pharaoh in the morning (Pharaoh's morning ritual at the river was likely religious) — is a public confrontation at Egypt's most sacred spot. The Egyptian magicians' replication of the plague (v.22) is contested: if all water is already blood, what do they use? Presumably residual clean water or wells; but their action is an imitation that cannot undo the damage, only match it.
14.literary_structure: The first plague establishes the full plague-narrative pattern: divine command → confrontation with Pharaoh at the Nile → staff-sign → plague → magicians replicate → Pharaoh hardens → plague ends → next plague announced. The 'by this you shall know that I am YHWH' formula (v.17) appears at the first plague and will recur throughout: the plagues are not random punishments but a structured self-introduction. The seven-day duration (v.25) is the first instance of a numbered duration — giving the plague a measured character, a defined period of divine judgment before the next escalation.
14.theological_application: Gill on 'by this thou shalt know that I am the Lord': 'by this miracle, this sign, this wonder, thou shalt have proof of it, experimental knowledge of it, that Jehovah, who has all power and perfection in him, and is the only true and living God, gave commission to Moses and Aaron to come unto him with such demands.' Matthew Henry: 'The Nile, which they had fondly trusted in, is made a stink to them. God can turn our comforts into crosses, and make that which we have most delighted in to be most grievous to us.' The first plague disciplines Egypt's idolatrous attachment to the Nile as the source of life by making the river itself the agent of death and corruption.
14.christology: The water-to-blood sign reverses at Cana: where Moses turned water to blood in judgment, Christ turned water to wine in blessing (John 2:1–11) — the first Exodus sign and the first Cana sign standing as judgment and grace, the same divine power deployed in opposite directions. Revelation 16:3–4 applies the blood-plague eschatologically: the bowls of wrath turn sea and rivers to blood in the final judgment. The Nile's defilement is the first of the Exodus judgments that cumulatively constitute a theology of divine justice against idolatry and oppression — a theology that the NT applies to the final judgment of Babylon (Revelation 16:6: 'they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and you have given them blood to drink').
